article.title: Use of drugs for hyperlipidaemia and diabetes and risk of primary and secondary brain tumours: nested case-control studies using the UK Clinical Practice Research Datalink (CPRD)
article.authors: Robinson JW, Martin R, Ozawa M, Elwenspoek MC, Redaniel MT, Kurian KM, Ben-Shlomo Y

study_type.notes: Nested case-control study using UK CPRD. Two separate studies: primary (1,950 cases, 7,791 controls) and secondary brain tumours (480 cases, 1,920 controls). Analysis of glitazone and fibrate exposure.

2.notes: Background: 'Previous studies have suggested that fibrates and glitazones may have a role in brain tumour prevention'

3.notes: Objective: 'We examined if there is support for these observations using primary care records from the UK CPRD'

4.notes: Study design: 'We conducted two nested case-control studies using primary and secondary brain tumours identified within CPRD between 2000 and 2016'

5.notes: Setting: 'Adults older than 18 years registered with a general practitioner in the UK contributing data to CPRD...brain tumours identified within CPRD between 2000 and 2016'

6a.notes: Not cohort study; case-control design described

6b.notes: Case definition and control selection: '7,496 individuals with any brain tumour (4,471 primary; 3,025 secondary)...After restricting cases and controls to those prescribed anti-diabetic/anti-hyperlipidaemic medication: 1,950 cases and 7,791 controls for fibrate analysis'

7.notes: Outcomes: primary and secondary brain tumours. Exposures: glitazone use, fibrate use. Confounders controlled through matching and antibiotic/antihyperglycemic cohort restriction

8.notes: Data sources: UK CPRD primary care records. Variables extracted: medication exposure duration, brain tumour diagnosis codes, covariates from medical records

9.notes: Bias addressing: 'We selected cases and controls among population of individuals treated with anti-diabetic/anti-hyperlipidaemic medication to reduce confounding by indication'

10.notes: No sample size justification or power calculation described

11.notes: Limited discussion of how duration of drug use was handled as continuous variable

12a.notes: Statistical methods: 'adjusted OR (aOR) 0.89 per year, 95% CI 0.80 to 0.98' - logistic regression with adjusted odds ratios

12b.notes: No explicit subgroup or interaction analyses reported

12c.notes: No discussion of missing data handling

12d.notes: Matching described: 'We selected cases and controls' but detailed matching strategy and its accounting in analysis not fully specified

13a.notes: Participant numbers: '7,496 individuals with brain tumour...restricted to those with medication: 1,950 cases/7,791 controls (fibrate); 480 cases/1,920 controls (glitazone)'

13b.notes: Limited information on reasons for restrictions or exclusions

14a.notes: No baseline characteristics table shown in provided text

14b.notes: No explicit reporting of missing data completeness

15.notes: Outcome data: glitazone exposure 'associated with reduced risk of primary (aOR 0.89 per year)...secondary (aOR 0.87 per year)...combined (aOR 0.88 per year)'

16a.notes: Adjusted estimates with precision: 'adjusted OR (aOR) 0.89 per year, 95% CI 0.80 to 0.98' for glitazone and primary tumours

16b.notes: Category boundaries not explicitly reported for continuous exposure

17.notes: No secondary subgroup or sensitivity analyses reported

18.notes: Key results: 'Longer exposure to glitazones was associated with reduced primary and secondary brain tumour risk'

19.notes: Limited discussion of study limitations in methods/abstract section

20.notes: Cautious interpretation: 'Further basic science and population-based research should explore this finding in greater detail'

22.notes: Funding source not stated in provided text
